entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: This is the transformation of the word

entities.name: āmnāya
entities.cite: those who know the tradition understand

axioms.name: vivartate
axioms.cite: Bhartrhari's own considered verb has been vivartate since 1.1

axioms.name: source
axioms.cite: from the metres, the hymns, first of all, this universe vyavartata, turned forth

axioms.name: teaches_cosmic_unity
axioms.cite: the verse is doing legitimating work: the extravagant claims of 1.119 and 1.121-123 are being shown to be not a grammarian's professional inflation but the oldest teaching of the tradition itself

denials.name: parinamate
denials.cite: parinama here sits in reported speech: it is the amnaya-knowers' formulation, and Bhartrhari's own considered verb has been vivartate since 1.1

claims.name: teaches_cosmic_unity
claims.cite: those who know the tradition understand

rejected_readings.label: endorse_parinama
rejected_readings.rendering: Bhartrhari endorses the doctrine that the word truly transforms into the universe, as the Vedic tradition teaches.
rejected_readings.why: Takes parinama as Bhartrhari's own philosophical position. The commentary is explicit: parinama is the amnaya-knowers' scriptural idiom; Bhartrhari's own verb, held consistently since 1.1, is vivartate (appearance without real transformation). This is the central distinction the commentary enforces.

rejected_readings.label: sabda_as_description
rejected_readings.rendering: The word is a descriptive property of the universe, not its generative source.
rejected_readings.why: Demotes sabda from absolute principle to property, reversing the asymmetry established by the axioms. An absolute can vivartate into a manifestation; a property cannot. This is a sort error that breaks the ontological hierarchy.

notes: The verse orchestrates two interpretive registers: the Vedic cosmogonic idiom (sabdasya parinamah, what the amnaya-knowers formulate) and Bhartrhari's Advaitic ontology (vivartate, what he actually holds since 1.1). The commentary's work is to justify citation of scriptural authority without endorsing scriptural language as truth-apt at the philosophical level. The contested note flags that readers divide on whether this is tacit endorsement or strategic appropriation; the contract encodes the reading the commentary explicitly states.
